question.text: Scenario: The Kapoor family consists of Mr. Rajiv (45, software engineer, gross salary Rs. 15 lakh p.a.) and Mrs. Neha (42, freelance designer, annual income Rs. 6 lakh). Their monthly breakdown is: Gross salary (Rajiv) Rs. 1,25,000, Freelance income (Neha) Rs. 50,000, Employer PF contribution Rs. 1,800, Employee PF contribution Rs. 1,800. Mandatory expenses: Tax deductions Rs. 12,000, Loan EMIs Rs. 35,000. Essential expenses: Groceries Rs. 15,000, Children's education Rs. 20,000, Rent Rs. 25,000, Utilities Rs. 8,000, Transportation Rs. 10,000. Discretionary expenses: Entertainment Rs. 8,000, Lifestyle Rs. 12,000, Annual holiday fund Rs. 5,000. Their assets include: Equity MFs Rs. 22 lakh, FDs Rs. 10 lakh, PPF Rs. 8 lakh, PF balance Rs. 14 lakh, Savings bank Rs. 2 lakh, Gold jewellery Rs. 6 lakh, Car (3 years old) Rs. 5 lakh. Liabilities: Home loan Rs. 18 lakh, Car loan Rs. 2,50,000. Question 3 of 5: The Kapoor family's leverage ratio and net worth are:

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Total assets = 56 + 11 = Rs. 67 lakh. Total liabilities = Home loan 18 + Car loan 2.50 = Rs. 20.50 lakh. Leverage ratio = 20.50/67 = 30.6%. Net worth = 67 - 20.50 = Rs. 46.50 lakh. Solvency ratio = 46.50/67 = 69.4%. **The Trap:** Rs. 50.25 lakh uses only financial assets in the denominator. Total assets includes both financial and physical assets. **Cross-Exam Context:** Leverage ratio and net worth are core X-A (Ch 3) calculations. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Leverage at 30.6% is moderate. As the home and car loans are repaid over time, the ratio will improve. Focus on not adding new debt.
